main_sections.title: The Tunnel
main_sections.prose_summary:
The Tunnel, written by Ruskin Bond, is a short story set in an Indian jungle. Suraj, a curious and adventurous boy, cycles out to a railway tunnel cut through a forested hill. He watches a steam engine roar through, and then walks the tunnel's dark, smoky interior just to experience it. On the hillside beyond, he glimpses a flash of orange and gold — a leopard. He meets the tunnel's sole watchman, Sunder Singh, an old man who feels a proprietary affection for the tunnel, the jungle, and even the leopard that periodically visits the area. Sunder Singh invites Suraj to return that evening to watch the night mail pass through.

Suraj comes back at dusk. The two sit together, drinking tea and listening to the forest's nighttime sounds — a barking deer, a fox, a nightjar, the creaking of trees. Sunder Singh makes his lamp-lit inspection of the tunnel. Inside, he hears something: a padded footfall, rustling leaves. He suspects the leopard is in the tunnel. With the night mail due in minutes, they cannot let it be killed. Suraj, despite his dry throat and fear, follows Sunder Singh into the dark. Their combined shouting drives the leopard out. The train thunders through, and the moment is exhilarating.

The story closes a week later: Suraj is on the night mail itself, accompanying his father to Delhi. In the darkness, he glimpses the distant lamp of Sunder Singh's hut and feels a quiet, warm connection to his friend — the lonely watchman who lights the dark for trains and leopards alike. The story's themes are curiosity, courage, unlikely friendship between generations, and a reverence for jungle life. Bond's prose captures the sensory richness of forest sounds, the power of a steam engine, and the intimacy of two people in darkness doing a brave, small thing together.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. What kind of an adventure activity would you like to be a part of? Choose the option(s) that best applies to you (questions 1–4 on personality type), and discuss with your classmates which label best applies to you: Bold Explorer, Imaginative Dreamer, Energetic Planner, or Thoughtful Creator.

question_bank.stem: How would you like to spend your weekends? (i) Trying new activities (ii) Reading a book (iii) Spending time with friends (iv) Learning something new
Which of these places would you prefer to explore? (i) Dense forests and clear streams (ii) Magical lands with fairies (iii) Busy cities with interesting people (iv) Quiet places to think and create
options: Quiet places to think and create

question_bank.stem: Which one of these activities would you prefer to do? (i) Climbing a mountain or exploring a jungle (ii) Finding a hidden treasure or discovering a secret world (iii) Solving puzzles or guessing riddles with friends (iv) Making something creative or building a model
options: Climbing a mountain or exploring a jungle
Finding a hidden treasure or discovering a secret world
Solving puzzles or guessing riddles with friends
Making something creative or building a model

question_bank.stem: What do you think about teamwork? (i) I love leading the team. (ii) I enjoy working with others. (iii) I prefer sharing ideas with friends. (iv) I like working alone.
options: I prefer sharing ideas with friends.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Section I — Let us discuss (after Part I of the story). Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Suraj compares the train to a magical creature. Pick the phrases that convey the meaning.
Suraj knew that the train was close by because ____________________.
What did Suraj see inside the tunnel?
Select the correct option. What does Sunder Singh convey about the tunnel? He says that ____________________ (it is damp and sticky inside / he has lent it to the Government).
options: it is damp and sticky inside
he has lent it to the Government

question_bank.stem: The watchman has created a system through which he informs the locomotive driver about any danger in the tunnel. Refer to the text and complete the flow chart. (Day: Walks the length of the tunnel → (i) _____ → No danger found → (ii) _____ OR Danger found → (iii) _____. Night: (iv) _____ → (v) _____ → Waves his lamp to warn the locomotive driver.)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Section II — Let us discuss (after Part II of the story). Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Sunder Singh knew that the leopard was in the tunnel because ____________.

Why do you think Suraj was unable to do any shouting in the tunnel in the beginning? (i) He was afraid of the leopard. (ii) He didn't want to disturb the leopard. (iii) He was trying to listen for the leopard. (iv) He didn't really believe Sunder Singh.
options: He was afraid of the leopard.
He didn't want to disturb the leopard.
He was trying to listen for the leopard.
He didn't really believe Sunder Singh.

question_bank.stem: What made the leopard disappear into the darkness?
How did Suraj know Sunder Singh was at the tunnel?
Why did Suraj's father take him along to Delhi?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section I. Read the following lines and answer the questions. (Extract 1: tunnel darkness passage; Extract 2: dialogue about the leopard in the tunnel.)

question_bank.stem: Write whether the following statement is true or false: Suraj looked back at the opening of the tunnel to check if the train was coming.

Suraj did not stop anywhere in the tunnel because _____________. A. there was nothing to see in the tunnel B. the tunnel was damp and sticky C. he was in a hurry to get out of the tunnel D. it was time for another train to come
options: there was nothing to see in the tunnel
the tunnel was damp and sticky
he was in a hurry to get out of the tunnel
it was time for another train to come

question_bank.stem: What does the sentence 'Till then, the cutting belonged to the jungle again' mean?
Suraj wanted to know the inside of the tunnel. What character trait of Suraj is highlighted here?
What was the watchman's concern? (from the leopard-in-tunnel extract)
Complete the sentence with a suitable reason. Sunder Singh was sure that the leopard in the tunnel would not attack him and Suraj because _____________.

Select the sentences that are true: A. Suraj wants to see the leopards in the jungle. B. Suraj is concerned about Sunder Singh's safety. C. The leopard in the tunnel attacks goats and stray dogs. D. Sunder Singh wants to protect Suraj. E. Sunder Singh knows all about the animals in the jungle.
Sunder Singh decides to take his axe with him. This tells us that he was _____________. A. violent B. cautious C. bold D. afraid

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What does Sunder Singh convey by saying that he has lent his tunnel to the Government?
According to Sunder Singh, 'It is safer in the forest than in the town'. What example does Sunder Singh give to support his opinion? What does this tell us about him?
Sunder Singh has become part of the jungle life. Support the view with a suitable reason.
Sunder Singh and Suraj form an unlikely friendship. What draws them together?
Why is Sunder Singh referred to as a 'firefly'?
Do you think Suraj is an adventure-loving boy? Give reasons.

listening.transcript_text: AUDIO GUIDE (Museum Treasure Hunt): Welcome to the City Museum. We start our tour at the entrance hall. Here you will find the information booth and a display devoted to the history of this museum. To the right of the Entrance Hall you will find the library. On the left is the museum shop. Go straight from the Entrance Hall till you reach the Art Gallery. The gallery is famous for its collection of rare paintings from various parts of India. There are three doors that lead out of the Art Gallery. The first door on the left leads to the Decorative Art Gallery, where you will find items like jewellery, carvings, and textiles from Medieval India. The next door is temporarily closed due to renovation work. The third door on the right leads to the Weapons Room, where you will find an extensive collection of Indian weapons including bows, swords, shields, and helmets. From the weapons room take a left turn. You will find the world-famous Musical Instruments Section, where you can find musical instruments from every country in the world. The second room to the left in this gallery will take you to the Auditorium. Hidden here is your treasure! All the best to all. May the best team win!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A famous museum in your town is organising a treasure hunt in the museum premises. You and your friends decide to participate in it. Each team is given an audio guide (recorded information) and a map to the museum. Listen to the audio recording and as you listen, mark your map with the route to take to find the treasure. (Rooms: library, auditorium, weapon room, art gallery, musical instruments hall.)

Anju is going on a trekking trip with her family during the summer vacation. They have reached the railway station. Anju calls the tour manager for directions to the trekking site. Work in pairs and have this conversation. Student A will play the role of Anju and mark the route on the map given below. Student B will play the role of the tour manager. Looking at the map, give directions to reach the trekking site from the railway station. You may use direction phrases from the box (turn left, turn right, keep going straight till, take the next left, take the next right, take the roundabout, take the second left, start from, on the opposite side, right around the corner, in front of, next to).

Write a descriptive paragraph about your experience of a train journey. Describe the sights, sounds, and feelings you experienced during the journey. Include details about the people around you, the scenery outside, and the atmosphere inside the train to make your paragraph vivid and engaging. Use these guidelines: (i) Start with how the journey began: boarding the train, finding your seat, and initial impressions. (ii) Describe the sounds: the whistle, chatter of passengers, rhythmic clatter of wheels. (iii) Mention the sights: changing scenery outside, hustle and bustle inside, interesting moments. (iv) Reflect on your feelings: excited, relaxed, curious?
Complete the following sentences using the suitable phrases from the text given in the box below (drifting smoke, approaching engine, sawing sound, flickering lights).

question_bank.stem: We could hear the ______________________ of the train long before it came into view at the station.
The street was completely dark but the little boy walked to his house guided by the ______________________ from the houses.
The ______________________ from the carpenter's workshop grew louder every hour.
From the balcony, she watched the ______________________ from her neighbour's chimney float across the backyard.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The text has many 'sound words'. Some of these words are given in Column 1 and their meanings in Column 2. Match the words with their meanings. You can use a dictionary. (creaking, chatter, hissing, rustle, puffing — matched with 5 meanings.)

question_bank.stem: Match the sound words (creaking, chatter, hissing, rustle, puffing) with their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Word|Meaning
creaking|making a long low sound when a door or wooden object moves or is moved
chatter|repeated series of short high sounds made by birds or monkeys
hissing|making a noise like a long 's' sound
rustle|a soft low, dry sound made by the movement of things such as leaves, paper, etc.
puffing|blowing with short, quick blasts, as the wind

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentences using the correct form of the sound words: (1) The steam engine reached the station __________________ black smoke into the sky. (2) Suddenly the snake raised its hood and made a __________________ noise. (3) When the wind blew, I heard the __________________ of the wooden roof. (4) He heard a __________________ of leaves behind him when he was walking in the woods. (5) I get disturbed by the continuous __________________ of the monkeys in the neighbourhood.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read how 'would' is used in the watchman's routine actions in the text. Notice that 'would' is used for habitual or routine actions in the past. 'Would' and 'used to' both describe repeated past actions, but 'used to' indicates an action that no longer happens.

question_bank.stem: Why is the word 'would' used in 'Every day, before the train came through, he would walk the length of the tunnel'? (i) to make a polite request (ii) to express a desire (iii) to talk about a habit in the past
options: to talk about a habit in the past

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentences using the verb in brackets with the correct form of 'used to' or 'would': When I was a child we used to live in a village near a forest. Back then, we (i) _____________ (grow) vegetables in our back garden. Every day we (ii) _____________ (get up) early and go for a walk. The weather in summer (iii) _____________ (be) really nice. There was a lake near the house and we (iv) _____________ (go) there every Sunday for a picnic. Winters (v) _____________ (be) rather cold. In the evenings, my father (vi) _____________ (tell) us incredible stories and we (vii) _____________ (listen) with admiration.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Use punctuation marks — brackets, semicolon, and colon — to punctuate the following paragraph about exploring Charminar, Hyderabad.

question_bank.stem: Use semicolons and colons to punctuate the paragraph: 'We explored the busy by-lanes of Charminar, the heart of Hyderabad it was an exciting day. We wanted to do a few things buy bangles, taste biryani, and purchase pearl earrings, which are special to this area...'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Nation's pride Atal Tunnel is 9.02 kilometres long. It runs under the Rohtang Pass and has been certified as the 'World's Longest Highway Tunnel above 10,000 Feet'. The history of Indian Railways spans over 165 years — first passenger train ran between Bori Bunder (Mumbai) and Thane on 16 April 1853. You may refer to the given link to know more about Indian Railways.
